question:
improper integral
\[
\int_{b}^{\infty} \left( \sqrt{\sqrt{x+a}-\sqrt{x}} -
\sqrt{\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{x-b}} \right)\,dx
\]
converge?
solution:
The integral converges iff $a=b$. The easiest proof uses
``big-O'' notation and the fact that $(1+x)^{1/2} = 1 + x/2 +
O(x^{2})$ for $|x|<1$. (Here $O(x^{2})$ means bounded by a constant
times $x^{2}$.)

So
\begin{align*}
\sqrt{x+a}-\sqrt{x} &= x^{1/2}(\sqrt{1+a/x} - 1) \\
&= x^{1/2}(1 + a/2x + O(x^{-2})),
\end{align*}
hence
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{x+a} - \sqrt{x}} = x^{1/4} (a/4x + O(x^{-2}))
\]
and similarly
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{x} - \sqrt{x-b}} = x^{1/4} (b/4x + O(x^{-2})).
\]
Hence the integral we're looking at is
\[
\int_{b}^{\infty} x^{1/4} ((a-b)/4x + O(x^{-2}))\,dx.
\]
The term $x^{1/4} O(x^{-2})$ is bounded by a constant times
$x^{-7/4}$, whose integral converges. Thus we only have to decide
whether $x^{-3/4} (a-b)/4$ converges. But $x^{-3/4}$ has divergent
integral, so we get convergence if and only if $a=b$ (in which case
the integral telescopes anyway).

descriptive_long.question:
improper integral
\[
\int_{paramtwo}^{\infty} \left( \sqrt{\sqrt{inputvar+paramone}-\sqrt{inputvar}} -
\sqrt{\sqrt{inputvar}-\sqrt{inputvar-paramtwo}} \right)\,dinputvar
\]
converge?
descriptive_long.solution:
The integral converges iff $paramone=paramtwo$. The easiest proof uses
``big-O'' notation and the fact that $(1+inputvar)^{1/2} = 1 + inputvar/2 +
O(inputvar^{2})$ for $|inputvar|<1$. (Here $O(inputvar^{2})$ means bounded by a constant
times $inputvar^{2}$.)

So
\begin{align*}
\sqrt{inputvar+paramone}-\sqrt{inputvar} &= inputvar^{1/2}(\sqrt{1+paramone/inputvar} - 1) \\
&= inputvar^{1/2}(1 + paramone/2inputvar + O(inputvar^{-2})),
\end{align*}
hence
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{inputvar+paramone} - \sqrt{inputvar}} = inputvar^{1/4} (paramone/4inputvar + O(inputvar^{-2}))
\]
and similarly
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{inputvar} - \sqrt{inputvar-paramtwo}} = inputvar^{1/4} (paramtwo/4inputvar + O(inputvar^{-2})).
\]
Hence the integral we're looking at is
\[
\int_{paramtwo}^{\infty} inputvar^{1/4} ((paramone-paramtwo)/4inputvar + O(inputvar^{-2}))\,dinputvar.
\]
The term $inputvar^{1/4} O(inputvar^{-2})$ is bounded by a constant times
$inputvar^{-7/4}$, whose integral converges. Thus we only have to decide
whether $inputvar^{-3/4} (paramone-paramtwo)/4$ converges. But $inputvar^{-3/4}$ has divergent
integral, so we get convergence if and only if $paramone=paramtwo$ (in which case
the integral telescopes anyway).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
improper integral
\[
\int_{pinecones}^{\infty} \left( \sqrt{\sqrt{sunflower+lanterns}-\sqrt{sunflower}} -
\sqrt{\sqrt{sunflower}-\sqrt{sunflower-pinecones}} \right)\,d sunflower
\]
converge?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The integral converges iff $lanterns=pinecones$. The easiest proof uses
``big-O'' notation and the fact that $(1+sunflower)^{1/2} = 1 + sunflower/2 +
O(sunflower^{2})$ for $|sunflower|<1$. (Here $O(sunflower^{2})$ means bounded by a constant
times $sunflower^{2}$.)

So
\begin{align*}
\sqrt{sunflower+lanterns}-\sqrt{sunflower} &= sunflower^{1/2}(\sqrt{1+lanterns/sunflower} - 1) \\
&= sunflower^{1/2}(1 + lanterns/2sunflower + O(sunflower^{-2})),
\end{align*}
hence
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{sunflower+lanterns} - \sqrt{sunflower}} = sunflower^{1/4} (lanterns/4sunflower + O(sunflower^{-2}))
\]
and similarly
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{sunflower} - \sqrt{sunflower-pinecones}} = sunflower^{1/4} (pinecones/4sunflower + O(sunflower^{-2})).
\]
Hence the integral we're looking at is
\[
\int_{pinecones}^{\infty} sunflower^{1/4} ((lanterns-pinecones)/4sunflower + O(sunflower^{-2}))\,d sunflower.
\]
The term $sunflower^{1/4} O(sunflower^{-2})$ is bounded by a constant times
$sunflower^{-7/4}$, whose integral converges. Thus we only have to decide
whether $sunflower^{-3/4} (lanterns-pinecones)/4$ converges. But $sunflower^{-3/4}$ has divergent
integral, so we get convergence if and only if $lanterns=pinecones$ (in which case
the integral telescopes anyway).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
improper integral
\[
\int_{ceilinglimit}^{\infty} \left( \sqrt{\sqrt{constantvalue+decremental}-\sqrt{constantvalue}} -
\sqrt{\sqrt{constantvalue}-\sqrt{constantvalue-ceilinglimit}} \right)\,dconstantvalue
\]
converge?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The integral converges iff $decremental=ceilinglimit$. The easiest proof uses
``big-O'' notation and the fact that $(1+constantvalue)^{1/2} = 1 + constantvalue/2 +
O(constantvalue^{2})$ for $|constantvalue|<1$. (Here $O(constantvalue^{2})$ means bounded by a constant
times $constantvalue^{2}$.)

So
\begin{align*}
\sqrt{constantvalue+decremental}-\sqrt{constantvalue} &= constantvalue^{1/2}(\sqrt{1+decremental/constantvalue} - 1) \\
&= constantvalue^{1/2}(1 + decremental/2constantvalue + O(constantvalue^{-2})),
\end{align*}
hence
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{constantvalue+decremental} - \sqrt{constantvalue}} = constantvalue^{1/4} (decremental/4constantvalue + O(constantvalue^{-2}))
\]
and similarly
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{constantvalue} - \sqrt{constantvalue-ceilinglimit}} = constantvalue^{1/4} (ceilinglimit/4constantvalue + O(constantvalue^{-2})).
\]
Hence the integral we\'re looking at is
\[
\int_{ceilinglimit}^{\infty} constantvalue^{1/4} ((decremental-ceilinglimit)/4constantvalue + O(constantvalue^{-2}))\,dconstantvalue.
\]
The term $constantvalue^{1/4} O(constantvalue^{-2})$ is bounded by a constant times
$constantvalue^{-7/4}$, whose integral converges. Thus we only have to decide
whether $constantvalue^{-3/4} (decremental-ceilinglimit)/4$ converges. But $constantvalue^{-3/4}$ has divergent
integral, so we get convergence if and only if $decremental=ceilinglimit$ (in which case
the integral telescopes anyway).

garbled_string.question:
improper integral
\[
\int_{fpdqneiu}^{\infty} \left( \sqrt{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla}-\sqrt{qzxwvtnp}} -
\sqrt{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp}-\sqrt{qzxwvtnp-fpdqneiu}} \right)\,d qzxwvtnp
\]
converge?
garbled_string.solution:
The integral converges iff $hjgrksla=fpdqneiu$. The easiest proof uses
``big-O'' notation and the fact that $(1+qzxwvtnp)^{1/2} = 1 + qzxwvtnp/2 +
O(qzxwvtnp^{2})$ for $|qzxwvtnp|<1$. (Here $O(qzxwvtnp^{2})$ means bounded by a constant
times $qzxwvtnp^{2}$.)

So
\begin{align*}
\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla}-\sqrt{qzxwvtnp} &= qzxwvtnp^{1/2}(\sqrt{1+hjgrksla/qzxwvtnp} - 1) \\
&= qzxwvtnp^{1/2}(1 + hjgrksla/2qzxwvtnp + O(qzxwvtnp^{-2})),
\end{align*}
hence
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla} - \sqrt{qzxwvtnp}} = qzxwvtnp^{1/4} (hjgrksla/4qzxwvtnp + O(qzxwvtnp^{-2}))
\]
and similarly
\[
\sqrt{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp} - \sqrt{qzxwvtnp-fpdqneiu}} = qzxwvtnp^{1/4} (fpdqneiu/4qzxwvtnp + O(qzxwvtnp^{-2})).
\]
Hence the integral we're looking at is
\[
\int_{fpdqneiu}^{\infty} qzxwvtnp^{1/4} ((hjgrksla-fpdqneiu)/4qzxwvtnp + O(qzxwvtnp^{-2}))\,d qzxwvtnp.
\]
The term $qzxwvtnp^{1/4} O(qzxwvtnp^{-2})$ is bounded by a constant times
$qzxwvtnp^{-7/4}$, whose integral converges. Thus we only have to decide
whether $qzxwvtnp^{-3/4} (hjgrksla-fpdqneiu)/4$ converges. But $qzxwvtnp^{-3/4}$ has divergent
integral, so we get convergence if and only if $hjgrksla=fpdqneiu$ (in which case
the integral telescopes anyway).

kernel_variant.question:
Let an integer $n\ge 3$ and a \emph{positive integer} $r\ge 2$ be fixed and put
\[
\lambda:=\frac1r\in(0,1),\qquad \rho:=1-\lambda .
\]

For $x>0$ introduce the $\lambda$-fractional kernel
\[
F_{r}(x):=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\Bigl(\,(x+a_{k})^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda},
\]
where  

$\bullet\;a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}>0$ are pairwise distinct,  

$\bullet\;(w_{1},\dots ,w_{n})\in\mathbf R^{\,n}$ and $(w_{k})$ is not the zero-vector.

For a complex Mellin parameter $\sigma$ put  
\[
I(\sigma):=\int_{0}^{\infty}x^{\sigma-1}F_{r}(x)\,dx\tag{$\star$}
\]
(the integral is taken over the maximal open vertical strip on which it converges absolutely).

Throughout, the symbol ``$\sim$'' denotes a \emph{complete} asymptotic expansion; as $x\to\infty$ the exponents drop by positive integers, whereas as $x\to0^{+}$ they increase by integer multiples of $\lambda$.

(a)\;Show that there exist computable constants $C_{j}(a),D_{j}(a)\;(j=0,1,2,\dots)$ such that, as $x\to\infty$ and $x\to0^{+}$ respectively,
\[
F_{r}(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}S_{j}\,x^{-\lambda\rho-j},\qquad 
  S_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}C_{j}(a_{k}),
\]
\[
F_{r}(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}T_{j}\,x^{j\lambda},\qquad\ 
  T_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}D_{j}(a_{k}).
\]

Define  
\[
m_{0}:=\sup\bigl\{j\ge 0\;:\;T_{0}=T_{1}=\dots =T_{j-1}=0\bigr\},
\qquad   
m_{\infty}:=\sup\bigl\{j\ge 0\;:\;S_{0}=S_{1}=\dots =S_{j-1}=0\bigr\},
\]
and note that if $m_{0},m_{\infty}<\infty$ then necessarily
$T_{m_{0}}\neq 0$ and $S_{m_{\infty}}\neq 0$.

Prove that the integral $(\star)$ converges absolutely if and only if
\[
-\;m_{0}\lambda\;<\;\operatorname{Re}\sigma\;<\;\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}.\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

(b)\;Analyse the two boundary lines.

(i)\;If $m_{0}<\infty$, show that for every $\sigma$ with
$\operatorname{Re}\sigma=-m_{0}\lambda$ the integral $(\star)$ diverges (indeed it already diverges at the integrable end $x\to 0^{+}$).  

(ii)\;If $m_{\infty}<\infty$, show that for every $\sigma$ with
$\operatorname{Re}\sigma=\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}$ the integral $(\star)$ diverges (already at $x\to\infty$).

(When $m_{0}= \infty$ or $m_{\infty}= \infty$ the corresponding boundary line is absent.)

(c)\;Assuming $(\dagger)$, prove that $I(\sigma)$ possesses a meromorphic continuation to the whole complex plane $\mathbf C$.  All poles are simple and lie on the two arithmetic progressions  
\[
\sigma=-j\lambda\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots),\qquad 
\sigma=\lambda\rho+j\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots).
\]

Let  
\[
j_{0}:=\min\{j\ge 0:\;T_{j}\neq 0\}\quad(\text{if such }j\text{ exists}),\qquad 
j_{1}:=\min\{j\ge 0:\;S_{j}\neq 0\}\quad(\text{if such }j\text{ exists}).
\]

Show that the left-most pole of $I(\sigma)$ is  
\[
\sigma_{\text{left}}
   =\begin{cases}
        -\,j_{0}\lambda,&\text{if }j_{0}\text{ exists},\\[4pt]
        \lambda\rho+j_{1},&\text{otherwise,}
     \end{cases}
\]
and compute the residues  
\[
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=-j\lambda}I(\sigma)=T_{j},\qquad
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=\lambda\rho+j}I(\sigma)=-\,S_{j}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
We abbreviate $\lambda:=1/r$, $\rho:=1-\lambda$ and $F(x):=F_{r}(x)$.

0.\;Local coefficients.  
For $j\ge 0$ define
\[
C_{j}(a):=\bigl[x^{-\lambda\rho-j}\bigr]\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}\quad(x\to\infty),
\]
\[
D_{j}(a):=\bigl[x^{\,j\lambda}\bigr]\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}\quad(x\to0^{+}),
\]
and put
\[
S_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\,C_{j}(a_{k}),\qquad
T_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\,D_{j}(a_{k}).\tag{0.1}
\]

Because $r\in\mathbf Z_{\ge2}$, $\lambda=1/r$; hence every exponent
$k+\ell\lambda$ ($k,\ell\in\mathbf N$) equals $j\lambda$ for the
integer $j:=kr+\ell$.  Consequently the ``grid'' that appears in the
binomial expansion near $x=0^{+}$ collapses to a \emph{single}
arithmetic progression and the coefficients $D_{j}(a)$ are
well-defined.

1.\;Complete asymptotic expansions of $F$.

(i)\;$x\to\infty$.  
Write $(x+a)^{\lambda}=x^{\lambda}(1+a/x)^{\lambda}$ and expand with the binomial series:
\[
(x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}=x^{\lambda}\bigl[(1+a/x)^{\lambda}-1\bigr]
      =\lambda a\,x^{\lambda-1}\Bigl[1-\frac{\rho a}{2x}+O\!\bigl(x^{-2}\bigr)\Bigr].
\]
Because $\lambda-1=-\rho$, raising to the power $\lambda$ gives
\[
\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}
      =(\lambda a)^{\lambda}x^{-\lambda\rho}
        \Bigl[1-\frac{\rho a}{2x}+O\!\bigl(x^{-2}\bigr)\Bigr]^{\lambda}
      \sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}C_{j}(a)\,x^{-\lambda\rho-j}.
\]
Summing over $k$ yields
\[
F(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}S_{j}\,x^{-\lambda\rho-j}.\tag{1.1}
\]

(ii)\;$x\to0^{+}$.  
Write $(x+a)^{\lambda}=a^{\lambda}(1+x/a)^{\lambda}$ and expand:
\[
(x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}=a^{\lambda}
      +\lambda a^{\lambda-1}x-x^{\lambda}+O\!\bigl(x^{2}\bigr).
\]
Raising to the power $\lambda$ and applying the binomial theorem twice gives the double sum
\[
\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}
     =a^{\lambda^{2}}
        \sum_{k,\ell\ge0}\binom{\lambda}{k+\ell}\binom{k+\ell}{k}
            \bigl(\lambda x/a\bigr)^{k}\Bigl(-x^{\lambda}/a^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\ell}.
\]
Replacing the pair $(k,\ell)$ by $j:=kr+\ell\;(=0,1,2,\dots)$ we can
re-index the sum and obtain
\[
\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}
      \sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}D_{j}(a)\,x^{j\lambda}, \qquad x\to0^{+},
\]
so that
\[
F(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}T_{j}\,x^{j\lambda}.\tag{1.2}
\]

2.\;Absolute convergence of $I(\sigma)$.

Define
\[
m_{0}:=\sup\{j\ge0:T_{0}=\dots =T_{j-1}=0\},\qquad 
m_{\infty}:=\sup\{j\ge0:S_{0}=\dots =S_{j-1}=0\}.
\]
If $m_{0}<\infty$ then $T_{m_{0}}\neq0$, and similarly
$S_{m_{\infty}}\neq0$ when $m_{\infty}<\infty$.

A.\;$x\to\infty$.  
By (1.1),
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim S_{m_{\infty}}\,x^{\sigma-1-\lambda\rho-m_{\infty}}.
\]
Hence $\int_{A}^{\infty}$ converges absolutely iff
\[
\operatorname{Re}\bigl(\sigma-1-\lambda\rho-m_{\infty}\bigr)<-1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
           \operatorname{Re}\sigma<\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}.\tag{2.1}
\]

B.\;$x\to0^{+}$.  
Using (1.2),
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim T_{m_{0}}\,x^{\sigma-1+m_{0}\lambda},
\]
so $\int_{0}^{B}$ converges absolutely iff
\[
\operatorname{Re}\bigl(\sigma-1+m_{0}\lambda\bigr)>-1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Re}\sigma>-m_{0}\lambda.\tag{2.2}
\]

C.\;Combining (2.1)-(2.2) gives exactly the strip ($\dagger$).
Conversely, violation of either inequality forces divergence, so ($\dagger$) is necessary and sufficient.  This completes part (a).

3.\;Behaviour on the boundary lines (part (b)).

(i)\;Suppose $m_{0}<\infty$ and $\operatorname{Re}\sigma=-m_{0}\lambda$.  
Near $x=0^{+}$,
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim T_{m_{0}}\,x^{-1},\qquad T_{m_{0}}\neq0,
\]
so $\int_{0}^{1}x^{-1}\,dx$ diverges; therefore $(\star)$ diverges.

(ii)\;Suppose $m_{\infty}<\infty$ and $\operatorname{Re}\sigma=\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}$.  
As $x\to\infty$,
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim S_{m_{\infty}}\,x^{-1},\qquad S_{m_{\infty}}\neq0,
\]
and $\int_{1}^{\infty}x^{-1}\,dx$ diverges; hence so does $(\star)$.
Thus part (b) is established.

4.\;Meromorphic continuation to $\mathbf C$ (part (c)).

Choose integers $N_{0},N_{\infty}\ge1$ and smooth cut-off functions
$\varphi_{0},\varphi_{\infty}$ with
\[
\varphi_{0}\equiv1\ \text{on }(0,1),\quad\operatorname{supp}\varphi_{0}\subset(0,2),
\]
\[
\varphi_{\infty}\equiv1\ \text{on }(1,\infty),\quad\operatorname{supp}\varphi_{\infty}\subset\bigl(\tfrac12,\infty\bigr).
\]
Define
\[
G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x):=F(x)
     -\varphi_{0}(x)\sum_{j=0}^{N_{0}-1}T_{j}x^{j\lambda}
     -\varphi_{\infty}(x)\sum_{j=0}^{N_{\infty}-1}S_{j}x^{-\lambda\rho-j}.
\tag{4.1}
\]
By (1.2)-(1.1) one has
\[
G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x)=O\!\bigl(x^{N_{0}\lambda}\bigr)\quad(x\to0^{+}),\qquad
G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x)=O\!\bigl(x^{-\lambda\rho-N_{\infty}}\bigr)\quad(x\to\infty).
\tag{4.2}
\]
Hence the Mellin transform
\[
\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(\sigma)
      :=\int_{0}^{\infty}x^{\sigma-1}G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x)\,dx\tag{4.3}
\]
is \emph{holomorphic} in the vertical strip
$-N_{0}\lambda<\operatorname{Re}\sigma<\lambda\rho+N_{\infty}$.
Because $N_{0},N_{\infty}$ are arbitrary, the overlapping family of strips covers $\mathbf C$, so the functions $\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}$ patch together to an \emph{entire} function.

Returning to $I(\sigma)$ we obtain
\[
I(\sigma)=\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(\sigma)
      +\sum_{j=0}^{N_{0}-1}T_{j}\,K_{0,j}(\sigma)
      +\sum_{j=0}^{N_{\infty}-1}S_{j}\,K_{\infty,j}(\sigma),\tag{4.4}
\]
where
\[
K_{0,j}(\sigma):=\int_{0}^{2}x^{\sigma+j\lambda-1}\varphi_{0}(x)\,dx,
\qquad
K_{\infty,j}(\sigma):=\int_{1/2}^{\infty}x^{\sigma-\lambda\rho-j-1}\varphi_{\infty}(x)\,dx.
\]

5.\;Location of poles and computation of residues.

Because $\varphi_{0}(0)=1$ and $\varphi_{\infty}(x)=1$ for $x\ge1$, standard Mellin asymptotics give
\[
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=-j\lambda}K_{0,j}(\sigma)=1,\qquad
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=\lambda\rho+j}K_{\infty,j}(\sigma)=-1.
\]
Every $K_{\bullet,j}$ is otherwise holomorphic.  Since $\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}$ is entire, the only singularities of $I(\sigma)$ are the simple poles
\[
\sigma=-j\lambda\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots),\qquad
\sigma=\lambda\rho+j\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots),\tag{5.1}
\]
with residues
\[
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=-j\lambda}I(\sigma)=T_{j},\qquad
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=\lambda\rho+j}I(\sigma)=-\,S_{j}.\tag{5.2}
\]

6.\;The left-most pole.

Let $j_{0},j_{1}$ be as in the problem statement.

$\bullet$ If $j_{0}$ exists, then $\sigma=-j_{0}\lambda$ lies strictly to the left of every pole in the second family, so it is the left-most pole with residue $T_{j_{0}}$.

$\bullet$ If no such $j_{0}$ exists, the first family is absent and the left-most pole is $\sigma=\lambda\rho+j_{1}$ with residue $-\,S_{j_{1}}$.

This completes the proof. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original oneŌĆōdimensional, two-parameter integral, the enhanced variant

ŌĆó works with an arbitrary number n Ōēź 3 of shifts and weights,  
ŌĆó introduces an extra Mellin parameter Žā, turning the problem into the study of an entire family of integrals,  
ŌĆó forces simultaneous control of the integrand at both 0 and Ōł×,  
ŌĆó requires the identification of higher-order ŌĆ£momentŌĆØ conditions (S_j, T_j) to describe cancellations,  
ŌĆó demands mastery of asymptotic expansions, Mellin transforms and meromorphic continuation to compute residues, and  
ŌĆó ties convergence questions to the analytic structure of I(Žā), not just to simple comparison tests.

These additional layers move the task well beyond straightforward power-series matching and necessitate a coordinated use of real analysis, complex analysis and asymptotic techniques, making the variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the current kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let an integer $n\ge 3$ and a rational number $r>1$ be fixed and set 

\[
\lambda:=\frac1r\in(0,1),\qquad \rho:=1-\lambda .
\]

For $x>0$ introduce the $\lambda$-fractional kernel  

\[
F_{r}(x):=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\Bigl(\,(x+a_{k})^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda},
\]

where  

$\bullet\;a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}>0$ are pairwise distinct,  

$\bullet\;(w_{1},\dots ,w_{n})\in\mathbf R^{\,n}$ and $(w_{k})$ is not the zero-vector.

For a complex Mellin parameter $\sigma$ put  

\[
I(\sigma):=\int_{0}^{\infty}x^{\sigma-1}F_{r}(x)\,dx\tag{$\star$}
\]
(the integral is understood in the maximal open vertical strip on which it converges absolutely).

Throughout, ``$\sim$'' means a complete asymptotic expansion; as $x\to\infty$ the powers drop by integers, whereas as $x\to0^{+}$ they increase by multiples of $\lambda$.

(a)\; Show that there exist computable constants $C_{j}(a),D_{j}(a)\;(j=0,1,2,\dots)$ such that, as $x\to\infty$ and $x\to0^{+}$ respectively,
\[
F_{r}(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}S_{j}x^{-\lambda\rho-j},\qquad 
  S_{j}:=\sum_{k}w_{k}C_{j}(a_{k}),
\]
\[
F_{r}(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}T_{j}x^{j\lambda},\qquad\ 
  T_{j}:=\sum_{k}w_{k}D_{j}(a_{k}).
\]

Define  
\[
m_{0}:=\sup\bigl\{j\ge 0\;:\;T_{0}=T_{1}=\dots =T_{j-1}=0\bigr\},
\qquad   
m_{\infty}:=\sup\bigl\{j\ge 0\;:\;S_{0}=S_{1}=\dots =S_{j-1}=0\bigr\},
\]
and note that if $m_{0},m_{\infty}<\infty$ then necessarily
$T_{m_{0}}\neq 0$ and $S_{m_{\infty}}\neq 0$.

Prove that the integral $(\star)$ converges absolutely iff 
\[
-\;m_{0}\lambda\;<\;\operatorname{Re}\sigma\;<\;\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}.\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

(b)\; Analyse the two boundary lines.

(i) If $m_{0}<\infty$, show that for every $\sigma$ with 
$\operatorname{Re}\sigma=-m_{0}\lambda$ the integral $(\star)$ diverges (indeed it already diverges at the integrable end $x\to0^{+}$).  

(ii) If $m_{\infty}<\infty$, show that for every $\sigma$ with 
$\operatorname{Re}\sigma=\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}$ the integral $(\star)$ diverges (already at $x\to\infty$).

(When $m_{0}= \infty$ or $m_{\infty}= \infty$ the corresponding boundary line is absent.)

(c)\; Assuming $(\dagger)$, prove that $I(\sigma)$ possesses a meromorphic continuation to the whole complex plane $\mathbf C$. All poles are simple and lie on the two arithmetic progressions  
\[
\sigma=-j\lambda\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots),\qquad 
\sigma=\lambda\rho+j\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots).
\]

Let  

\[
j_{0}:=\min\{j\ge 0:\;T_{j}\neq 0\}\quad(\text{if such }j\text{ exists}),\qquad 
j_{1}:=\min\{j\ge 0:\;S_{j}\neq 0\}\quad(\text{if such }j\text{ exists}).
\]

Show that the left-most pole of $I(\sigma)$ is  

\[
\sigma_{\text{left}}
   =\begin{cases}
        -\,j_{0}\lambda,&\text{if }j_{0}\text{ exists},\\[4pt]
        \lambda\rho+j_{1},&\text{otherwise,}
     \end{cases}
\]
and compute the residues  
\[
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=-j\lambda}I(\sigma)=T_{j},\qquad
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=\lambda\rho+j}I(\sigma)=S_{j}.
\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We abbreviate $\lambda:=1/r$, $\rho:=1-\lambda$ and $F(x):=F_{r}(x)$.  

0.\;Local coefficients.  
For $j\ge 0$ define  
\[
C_{j}(a):=\bigl[x^{-\lambda\rho-j}\bigr]\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}\quad(x\to\infty),
\]
\[
D_{j}(a):=\bigl[x^{\,j\lambda}\bigr]\Bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\lambda}\quad(x\to0^{+}),
\]
and put  
\[
S_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\,C_{j}(a_{k}),\qquad
T_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\,D_{j}(a_{k}).\tag{0.1}
\]

1.\;Complete asymptotic expansions of $F$.

(i)\;$x\to\infty$.  
Write $(x+a)^{\lambda}=x^{\lambda}(1+a/x)^{\lambda}$ and use the binomial series:
\[
(x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}=x^{\lambda}\bigl[(1+a/x)^{\lambda}-1\bigr]
      =\lambda a\,x^{\lambda-1}\Bigl[1-\frac{\rho a}{2x}+O(x^{-2})\Bigr].
\]
Because $\lambda-1=-\rho$, raising to the power $\lambda$ gives
\[
\bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\bigr)^{\lambda}
      =(\lambda a)^{\lambda}x^{-\lambda\rho}
        \Bigl[1-\frac{\rho a}{2x}+O(x^{-2})\Bigr]^{\lambda}
      \sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}C_{j}(a)\,x^{-\lambda\rho-j}.
\]
Summing over $k$ yields  
\[
F(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}S_{j}\,x^{-\lambda\rho-j}.\tag{1.1}
\]

(ii)\;$x\to0^{+}$.  
Write $(x+a)^{\lambda}=a^{\lambda}(1+x/a)^{\lambda}$ and expand:
\[
(x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}=a^{\lambda}
      +\lambda a^{\lambda-1}x-x^{\lambda}+O(x^{2}).
\]
Since $0<\lambda<1$, one has $x=o(x^{\lambda})$ near $0$, so the dominant pieces are $a^{\lambda}$ and $-x^{\lambda}$.  Factoring $a^{\lambda}$ and expanding again,
\[
\bigl((x+a)^{\lambda}-x^{\lambda}\bigr)^{\lambda}
     =a^{\lambda^{2}}
        \Bigl[1-\bigl(x/a\bigr)^{\lambda}
                 +\lambda(x/a)+O(x^{2})\Bigr]^{\lambda}
     \sim a^{\lambda^{2}}\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}E_{j}(a)\,x^{j\lambda},
\]
so that  
\[
F(x)\sim\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}T_{j}\,x^{j\lambda}.\tag{1.2}
\]

2.\;Absolute convergence of $I(\sigma)$.

Define  
\[
m_{0}:=\sup\{j\ge0:T_{0}=\dots =T_{j-1}=0\},\qquad 
m_{\infty}:=\sup\{j\ge0:S_{0}=\dots =S_{j-1}=0\}.
\]
If $m_{0}<\infty$ then $T_{m_{0}}\neq0$, and similarly
$S_{m_{\infty}}\neq0$ when $m_{\infty}<\infty$.

A.\;$x\to\infty$.  
By (1.1),
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim S_{m_{\infty}}\,x^{\sigma-1-\lambda\rho-m_{\infty}}.
\]
Hence $\int_{A}^{\infty}$ converges absolutely iff  
\[
\operatorname{Re}(\sigma-1-\lambda\rho-m_{\infty})<-1\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
           \operatorname{Re}\sigma<\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}.\tag{2.1}
\]

B.\;$x\to0^{+}$.  
Using (1.2),
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim T_{m_{0}}\,x^{\sigma-1+m_{0}\lambda},
\]
so $\int_{0}^{B}$ converges absolutely iff  
\[
\operatorname{Re}(\sigma-1+m_{0}\lambda)>-1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 
\operatorname{Re}\sigma>-m_{0}\lambda.\tag{2.2}
\]

C.\;Combining (2.1)-(2.2) gives precisely the strip ($\dagger$).
Conversely, violation of either inequality forces divergence, so ($\dagger$) is necessary and sufficient.  This completes part (a).

3.\;Behaviour on the boundary lines (part (b)).

(i)\;Suppose $m_{0}<\infty$ and $\operatorname{Re}\sigma=-m_{0}\lambda$.  
Near $x=0^{+}$,
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim T_{m_{0}}\,x^{-1},\qquad T_{m_{0}}\neq0,
\]
so $\int_{0}^{1}x^{-1}\,dx$ diverges; therefore $(\star)$ diverges.

(ii)\;Suppose $m_{\infty}<\infty$ and $\operatorname{Re}\sigma=\lambda\rho+m_{\infty}$.  
As $x\to\infty$,
\[
x^{\sigma-1}F(x)\sim S_{m_{\infty}}\,x^{-1},\qquad S_{m_{\infty}}\neq0,
\]
and $\int_{1}^{\infty}x^{-1}\,dx$ diverges; hence so does $(\star)$.
Thus part (b) is established.

4.\;Meromorphic continuation to $\mathbf C$ (part (c)).

Pick integers $N_{0},N_{\infty}\ge1$ and smooth cut-off functions 
$\varphi_{0},\varphi_{\infty}$ with  
\[
\varphi_{0}\equiv1\ \text{on }(0,1),\quad\operatorname{supp}\varphi_{0}\subset(0,2),
\]
\[
\varphi_{\infty}\equiv1\ \text{on }(1,\infty),\quad\operatorname{supp}\varphi_{\infty}\subset\bigl(\tfrac12,\infty\bigr).
\]
Define
\[
G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x):=F(x)
     -\varphi_{0}(x)\sum_{j=0}^{N_{0}-1}T_{j}x^{j\lambda}
     -\varphi_{\infty}(x)\sum_{j=0}^{N_{\infty}-1}S_{j}x^{-\lambda\rho-j}.
\tag{4.1}
\]
By (1.2)-(1.1) one has  
\[
G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x)=O\bigl(x^{N_{0}\lambda}\bigr)\quad(x\to0^{+}),\qquad
G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x)=O\bigl(x^{-\lambda\rho-N_{\infty}}\bigr)\quad(x\to\infty).
\tag{4.2}
\]
Hence the Mellin transform  
\[
\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(\sigma)
      :=\int_{0}^{\infty}x^{\sigma-1}G_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(x)\,dx\tag{4.3}
\]
is an entire function in the vertical strip
$-N_{0}\lambda<\operatorname{Re}\sigma<\lambda\rho+N_{\infty}$.
Because $N_{0},N_{\infty}$ are arbitrary, $\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}$
extends to an entire function on $\mathbf C$.

A direct calculation gives
\[
I(\sigma)=\mathcal M_{N_{0},N_{\infty}}(\sigma)
      +\sum_{j=0}^{N_{0}-1}\frac{T_{j}}{\sigma+j\lambda}
      +\sum_{j=0}^{N_{\infty}-1}\frac{S_{j}}{\sigma-(\lambda\rho+j)}.
\tag{4.4}
\]
Increasing $N_{0}$ or $N_{\infty}$ alters only the entire part, so
(4.4) furnishes a meromorphic continuation of $I$ to $\mathbf C$ whose
poles are exactly
\[
\sigma=-j\lambda\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots),\qquad
\sigma=\lambda\rho+j\quad(j=0,1,2,\dots),\tag{4.5}
\]
all simple.  Part (c) is proved.

5.\;Residues and the left-most pole.

From (4.4)
\[
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=-j\lambda}I(\sigma)=T_{j},\qquad
\operatorname*{Res}_{\sigma=\lambda\rho+j}I(\sigma)=S_{j}.\tag{5.1}
\]
Let $j_{0},j_{1}$ be as in the problem statement.  If $j_{0}$ exists,
then $\sigma=-j_{0}\lambda$ lies strictly to the left of every pole in
the second family and is therefore the left-most pole, with residue
$T_{j_{0}}$.  If no such $j_{0}$ exists, the first family is absent and
the left-most pole is $\sigma=\lambda\rho+j_{1}$ with residue
$S_{j_{1}}$. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:

ŌĆó works with an arbitrary number n Ōēź 3 of shifts and weights,  
ŌĆó introduces an extra Mellin parameter Žā, turning the problem into the study of an entire family of integrals,  
ŌĆó forces simultaneous control of the integrand at both 0 and Ōł×,  
ŌĆó requires the identification of higher-order ŌĆ£momentŌĆØ conditions (S_j, T_j) to describe cancellations,  
ŌĆó demands mastery of asymptotic expansions, Mellin transforms and meromorphic continuation to compute residues, and  
ŌĆó ties convergence questions to the analytic structure of I(Žā), not just to simple comparison tests.

These additional layers move the task well beyond straightforward power-series matching and necessitate a coordinated use of real analysis, complex analysis and asymptotic techniques, making the variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the current kernel version.
